critic_raw:
1. The fairness definition is operationally circular and untested against the stated threat. "Perfect fairness" is defined as selecting balanced outcomes with respect to sensitive attributes, but the experiment never verifies that the algorithm's balance property holds under the actual distributions of the test datasets. If the datasets have varying group sizes, the algorithm may satisfy the formal definition vacuously (e.g., by never selecting any candidate from a minority group if the pool is too small) while failing the spirit of fair selection. No analysis checks whether the realized selection rates equal the theoretical guarantees.

2. Reproduction from scratch does not distinguish algorithmic fidelity from implementation variance. The paper claims to reproduce algorithms from descriptions, but the reproduction task does not establish that the implemented algorithms are equivalent to the originals. Small deviations in tie-breaking, probability estimation, or threshold handling can produce qualitatively different outcomes on finite samples. Without a formal equivalence check (e.g., matching outputs on shared synthetic instances with known properties), the reproduction only establishes that "something with similar structure" was run — not that the original claims were validated.

3. Performance evaluation in the Prophet setting conflates expected-value maximization with the stated fairness objective. The average value of the selected candidate is used as the performance metric, but this metric directly rewards algorithms that select high-value candidates — which may systematically favor majority groups if value correlates with group membership in the data. The paper does not test whether maximizing value and achieving balance are in tension, or whether the claimed "comparable performance" holds when value is decorrelated from sensitive attributes.

4. The Secretary and Prophet settings are evaluated on "various datasets" with no specification of how those datasets were chosen or whether they span the realistic operating regime. If datasets were selected post-hoc to show favorable results, the generalization claim is unfounded. If all datasets are synthetic, the comparison is inherently governed by the synthetic generation assumptions, not real-world candidate distributions. The experiment does not test robustness to adversarial orderings or skewed value distributions, which are the settings where online selection algorithms most commonly fail in practice.
all_issues_raised: Fairness definition is verified only formally, not against realized selection rates under realistic group-size imbalance
Reproduction from scratch cannot establish algorithmic equivalence without matching outputs on shared test instances
Average-value performance metric conflates value maximization with fairness, potentially rewarding majority-group bias
Dataset selection criteria undisclosed, allowing post-hoc cherry-picking that would inflate apparent generalization
Robustness to adversarial candidate orderings and skewed value distributions is never tested
Perfect fairness claim is definition-specific; the design does not test whether competing fairness definitions are simultaneously satisfied or in conflict with competitive ratio guarantees
Comparison baselines may have been selected because they are known to be weak or unfair, making the comparison uninformative about the true performance frontier
Performance metric conflates hindsight-optimal with distribution-optimal; the exact definition of optimal candidate is underspecified relative to the sequential decision constraint
Fairness guarantee may degrade under skewed protected attribute distributions; dataset distributional properties are not reported and stress-testing under skew is absent
Reproducibility from scratch without specifying available artifacts (pseudocode vs prose description) makes implementation discrepancies unattributable to algorithm vs implementation choices
Fairness is a definitional guarantee by construction, not an empirically tested property — evaluating balance when the algorithm is designed to produce balance is vacuous
No comparison against unconstrained optimal algorithm, so the utility cost of the fairness constraint is unquantified
Dataset dependence not characterized: performance claims may not hold under adversarial group size ratios or correlated quality-attribute distributions
Reproduction lacks control over candidate arrival order, which is a critical source of variance for online selection algorithms
Secretary and Prophet results conflated in unified claims despite having fundamentally different mathematical structures and fairness-efficiency tradeoffs
